name: Mandana Misra
dates_notes: Contemporary with Śaṅkara; legendary debate may be later hagiography but textual contemporaneity is solid.

lineage_polemical.note: Maṇḍana's bhāvanā / jīva-as-locus-of-avidyā position is structurally opposed to Sureśvara's jñāna-only / Brahman-as-locus-of-avidyā stream.

core_thesis: Maṇḍana defends *advaita* in a distinctive register shaped by his prior Mīmāṃsaka training. *Brahman* is one, of the nature of consciousness-bliss, and *avidyā* — the principle of apparent multiplicity — is located in the *jīvas* (not in *brahman*), is plural (one per *jīva*), and is *anirvacanīya* (indeterminable as either real or unreal). Knowledge of *brahman* arises from *mahāvākya*-cognition, but the cognition does not by itself terminate ignorance: liberating realization requires *prasaṅkhyāna* — sustained meditative *bhāvanā* on the truth, prolonged and repeated until the *aparokṣa* (immediate) realization stabilizes. This *jñāna-bhāvanā* doctrine and the *jīva*-locus account of *avidyā* are the two doctrinal hinges that distinguish the eventual *Bhāmatī* sub-school (transmitted via Vācaspati) from the Sureśvara-Vivaraṇa stream, which holds *avidyā* as one and located in *brahman* and which treats *vākya*-cognition as itself sufficient. Maṇḍana also takes *śabda* — following Bhartṛhari — as a primary route into *brahman*, integrating *śabda-advaita* into Vedānta proper.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored. The traditional identification of pre-conversion Maṇḍana with Sureśvara is disputed (rejected by Hiriyanna; affirmed by traditional sectarian sources).

ascription_evidence.description: Modern consensus accepts Maṇḍana's authorship of the Brahma-Siddhi.

engaged_works.summary: An independent *prakaraṇa* in four chapters (*kāṇḍas*): *Brahma-Kāṇḍa* (the nature of *brahman* and the *jīva*'s relation to it), *Tarka-Kāṇḍa* (epistemological defense — *pramāṇa* analysis, refutation of *bhedavāda*), *Niyoga-Kāṇḍa* (engagement with Mīmāṃsaka *niyoga*/injunction theory and the relation of Vedic action to liberating knowledge), *Siddhi-Kāṇḍa* (the consummation: realization, *prasaṅkhyāna*-method, *jīvanmukti*). The *prasaṅkhyāna-vāda* — that Upaniṣadic *mahāvākya*-cognition must be prolonged into meditative *bhāvanā* before liberating realization arises — is the work's signature, and is precisely what Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi will reject. The text-historical question of whether Maṇḍana is identical to the pre-conversion Sureśvara/Viśvarūpa is contested: traditional Advaita sectarian sources affirm the identification, while Hiriyanna and most modern academics (Thrasher, Allen Thrasher's monograph) reject it on grounds of doctrinal divergence between the Brahma-Siddhi and Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi.
